nodes.text: Harmony Creek Estates was incorporated as a planned residential community in 1987. The name was chosen by a committee. The creek, which is real, runs behind the Albertsons on Gallatin Avenue and smells, in summer, of warm algae and whatever the storm drain by the middle school contributes. The harmony is aspirational.
Population: 14,400. Median household income: $74,200. Number of HOA-compliant mailboxes: 4,891. Number of residents currently awake at 6:12 on a Sunday morning in early June: fewer than you'd think.
Harmony Creek Estates

The sprinkler systems on Meadowlark Circle come on at 6:12, as they do every Sunday and Thursday, in accordance with the Stage 2 Water Conservation Schedule maintained by the city of Harmony Creek and the Harmony Creek Homeowners Association jointly, in a spirit of cooperative governance that both parties describe, in their respective newsletters, as a model for the region.

The sprinklers activate in sequence, lot by lot, traveling east down the block. Number 1402 goes first. Then 1408. Then 1414. Then 1420. Then 1428, which is the Miller house, where the head on the left corner of the yard has a hairline crack in the housing and throws water in a thin, surgical arc that hits the sidewalk at a forty-degree angle and has, over the past two summers, etched a faint grey stripe into the concrete that the HOA has not yet formally noticed.
Sam Miller's Bedroom
Sam Miller is not asleep.

She has been not asleep since approximately 4:40, when she woke from a dream she cannot fully recall — something with water, something with a door that wouldn't close — and lay in the dark with the specific awareness that something is wrong.
Something is wrong.

She has not been able to locate the wrongness. This is the most unsettling part. Sam Miller is a person who locates things. It is, according to her father, her defining characteristic, delivered as a compliment that functions as a warning.

She lies on her back. The ceiling fan turns. The light through her curtains shifts from black to grey to the particular color of early June at 6:13 in a Texas suburb, which is the color of a sheet of paper that has been left on a desk near a window for a few months too long.
Diego hasn't texted back.

She had texted him at 11:30 the previous night. She had texted him again at 12:04. The messages show as delivered. They do not show as read. This is not, by itself, unusual. Diego Ramos is not a person who reads texts promptly. Diego Ramos is a person who will sometimes not read a text for sixteen hours and then respond with a single letter, the letter being, usually, k.
But something is wrong.

She sits up. Outside, the cracked sprinkler head throws its angled jet across the sidewalk. She can hear it. She watches the stripe of light on her wall until it shifts enough to mean it is fully morning, and then she gets up.
Diego Ramos's Bedroom
Diego Ramos's bedroom at 892 Ashberry Drive is empty.

The bed has been slept in but not recently. The sheets are pushed to one side. On the floor near the desk there is an open duffel bag — the green one he has had since eighth grade, with the broken zipper on the front pocket that he fixed once with a safety pin and then the safety pin was lost and he never fixed it again. The bag is half-packed. Three shirts, a phone charger, a pair of boots.

On the desk: a water glass with a finger of water left in it. A textbook for a class he is not taking anymore. A photograph of Sam that he printed at the Walgreens on Fifth, tucked into the corner of the mirror, which is the only decoration in the room besides a periodic table he has had since seventh grade and a calendar he stopped updating in March.
The photograph is still there. The duffel bag is still there. The water glass is still there. Diego Ramos is not.
Graciela's Bedroom

Down the hall, Graciela Ramos — Diego's grandmother, seventy-one years old, a person who has survived four hurricanes and two husbands and a son who made choices she still has not found words for — is sitting on the edge of her bed in the dark, holding a rosary she is not praying with.
She heard him leave. She heard the truck start. She heard the sound of the engine getting smaller until it was nothing.
She did not get up.

She is not sure, sitting here now with the rosary, why she did not get up. She tells herself it was because she knew she could not stop him. She tells herself it was because a boy his age has to be allowed to go. She tells herself a number of things that are all true and none of which are the whole truth.
He is his father's son. And I could not stop his father either.
She will call Sam later. Not yet. She does not know what she will say when she calls. She is waiting for the words to arrive.
The Darkroom
At 3017 Verbena Way, in a converted half-bath off the garage that still has the original avocado tile from 1974, Rick Salazar is developing photographs.

Rick Salazar is seventeen. Rick Salazar is the editor of the Harmony Creek High School newspaper, The Meadowlark, a position he takes more seriously than anyone has taken it in the paper's thirty-year history, and which has not yet caused him to make a single friend he did not already have, and which he would not trade for anything.

The photographs he is developing are from Thursday. He had his camera at the corner of Gallatin and Fifth, shooting the new construction on the NexCorp lot. He does not know yet what is on them. That is the thing about film. You have to wait.
He slides the paper into the developer tray. He uses the tongs. He watches.

The image comes up slowly, the way they always do — a grey wash that becomes shapes that becomes a photograph. The NexCorp van is in the frame. Two men in navy polo shirts. And, at the edge of the frame, half-cut-off by the border, a black pickup truck with Louisiana plates.
Huh.
He pins it on the line. He makes a note in his notebook. He will look at it again when the light is better.
Maya Daigle's Bedroom

Maya Daigle is awake because Maya Daigle is always awake at 6:16. Maya Daigle has been waking at 6:16 since she was nine years old and her mother told her that the first hour of the day belongs to you and no one can take it. Her mother was right about very little, but she was right about this.
Maya is sitting cross-legged on her bed with a spiral notebook open in her lap. She is writing in her rapid, cramped hand that no one else can fully read, which is by design.

Sam said Diego hadn't come home. Sam said she wasn't worried but she texted me at midnight which is how I know she is worried. Sam worries in straight lines — she goes from one point to another without acknowledging the distance between them. The distance is always where the thing is.

Things I know: Diego was working on something. Diego stopped talking about what he was working on, which means it got to the part where it got serious. Diego did not tell Sam it got serious, which means he was protecting her, which means he thought she would try to stop him. Sam would try to stop him if she thought it was dangerous. Diego thought it was dangerous. Diego left anyway.
She underlines the last line twice.
She writes: Who knew?
She writes: Ben.
Pit Stop Diner

Ben Achterberg is in the back office of the Pit Stop Diner at 06:17 on a Sunday morning because the Pit Stop Diner opens at seven and Ben Achterberg is the kind of person who arrives an hour early, not because he has to, but because the hour before the diner opens is the only hour in the day that belongs entirely to the diner and not to the people in it.
He is sitting at the desk that used to be Rick's father's desk, in the chair that has been reupholstered twice, looking at a legal pad.
On the legal pad, he has written, in his careful block letters:

WHAT I KNOW
——————————
NexCorp bought the Gallatin lot in March.
NexCorp bought the Henderson property in April.
NexCorp is building something they haven't filed complete permits for.
Diego was looking into the permits.
Diego is gone.
He has crossed out the last line and rewritten it below:

Diego left. Diego left on his own. Nobody made Diego leave.
(I don't know this. I don't know this yet.)

He puts the pen down. He picks it up again. He is fifty-four years old and has owned this diner for eleven years and before that worked in it for six, and in those seventeen years he has developed a very precise sense of when a situation is about to become a situation.
This is a situation.
The Water Tower

The Harmony Creek water tower stands at the south end of Gallatin Avenue, behind the NexCorp lot, which is currently enclosed in a chain-link fence with green privacy screen. The water tower is sixty-two feet tall. It was painted in 2019 with the words HARMONY CREEK in blue block letters and a small rendering of a creek that does not resemble the actual creek.
At 6:19, a white NexCorp Residential Solutions van is parked beside the fence. The engine is off. No one gets out. The van has been there since 6:04.

A man named Henderson — F. Thomas Henderson, thirty-eight, NexCorp field coordinator, a person who has worked for NexCorp for nine years and for the six years before that worked for a company that NexCorp acquired — is in the passenger seat, on his phone.

The Ramos kid is gone. Good. That's one thing. The Miller girl is a different problem. Chief Miller's daughter, that's — that's a different problem. Need to know if she's talked to anyone. Need to know what the kid told her before he left.
He sends a text. He waits. The reply comes in forty seconds.
He reads it. He types a reply. He puts the phone in his breast pocket and looks at the green privacy screen on the fence, behind which the machines have already begun their work for the day.
It'll be fine. It's always fine. These towns always end up fine.
He has told himself this in seven towns. He is telling himself this in the eighth.
Chief Miller's Kitchen

Chief Dale Miller — Police Chief of Harmony Creek, thirteen years in the role, former marine, father of one, a man whose defining characteristic is, according to his daughter, that he is always the last person in any room to say what he actually thinks — is standing at his kitchen counter at 6:24, waiting for his coffee to finish, reading a text message.
The text is from a number he does not have saved. It reads:

Chief — this is Carla Reyes at the Statesman. I know it's early. I've been working on something I think you need to know about before it goes to print. It involves NexCorp and a series of property acquisitions in your town going back to January. I have documentation. I'd like to talk to you before we publish. This is a courtesy call, not a request for comment. Please call me back.
He reads it twice. He puts the phone on the counter. He pours his coffee.

He picks the phone back up. He does not call the number. He types, instead: Understood. Give me the week. He sends it. He does not know if she will give him the week. He is buying time, which is the thing he does when he does not yet know what he thinks.

Sammy won't let it go. She's been asking about NexCorp since March. I told her it was nothing. I told her I'd look into it. I looked into it and I found what I expected to find, which is that there is nothing on paper that shouldn't be there, and I know enough about how these things work to know that means either there really is nothing, or the paper is very clean.

And Diego Ramos is missing. And Graciela Ramos has not called me yet, which means she either doesn't know or doesn't want to make it official. And I have a reporter from the Statesman texting me at six in the morning.
He stands at the counter. He drinks his coffee. He is thinking very carefully about what he is going to do and in what order he is going to do it, which is how he does everything.

At 6:28, the sprinklers on Meadowlark Circle finish their cycle. The cracked head on the Miller lawn sputters once and goes still, leaving a last thin arc of water that catches the early light for a moment and then falls.
Down the street, a garage door opens. A dog barks twice and stops. Someone's car alarm chirps once, a half-second confusion, and silences.

The town of Harmony Creek, Texas, is waking up. It is doing so in the manner of a town that has not yet been told it needs to be worried — in increments, in the small accumulated sounds of people beginning their Sunday, unaware that the Sunday they are beginning has already been shaped, in the dark, by decisions made without them.

The creek behind the Albertsons moves at the pace it always moves. The water tower stands at the south end of Gallatin. The green privacy screen on the NexCorp fence moves slightly in the early wind and settles.
In the kitchen on Meadowlark Circle, a voice calls up the stairs.
Sam. You up?
Yeah.
Come down when you're ready. I want to talk to you about something.
A pause. The sound of feet on the floor above. A door.
Okay.

The word okay, in Sam Miller's voice, at 6:28 on this particular Sunday morning, carries in it — if you know her, and her father knows her — the full weight of a person who has been awake since 4:40, who has already decided something is wrong, and who has been waiting since then for someone else to say it out loud.
